title: CSR: Parasites & Prevention (Client-Facing)
description: A client-facing CSR course on common parasite-prevention conversations, fecal samples, heartworm prevention basics, flea/tick questions, and knowing when to route medical questions.

blocks.prompt: A client asks if a positive fecal means their pet is contagious to kids.

options.text: Guess an answer.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Route medical and public-health questions to the medical team.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Tell them parasites are never a concern.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: CSRs may explain sample needs, appointment prep, and general prevention purpose. They should not diagnose parasites, interpret test results, prescribe products, or give treatment instructions.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Clinic process, sample needs, and general prevention purpose.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Educate on process; route medical questions.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A client says the pet looks healthy and asks why a fecal is needed.

options.text: Say “because we said so.”
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Explain that some parasites are not obvious and the sample helps screen.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Tell them the pet definitely has worms.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Fecal testing screens for intestinal parasites that may not be obvious at home. A simple explanation helps clients understand why the clinic asks for samples.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Diagnosis or reassurance beyond the role.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Screens for parasites; doctor reviews results.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A client missed several prevention doses and asks what to do.

options.text: Tell them to double the next dose.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Route to the medical team.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Say it does not matter.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Heartworm testing and prevention are part of preventive-care workflows. The CSR can explain appointment or refill process, but product choice, missed doses, reactions, and dosing questions should be routed.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Missed doses, reactions, product safety, and treatment questions.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.

blocks.prompt: A client asks if they can use dog flea medication on their cat.

options.text: Say yes because it treats fleas.
options.feedback: This response either misses urgency, sounds dismissive, crosses scope, or creates avoidable workflow risk.

options.text: Tell them not to use unapproved products and route to medical team.
options.feedback: Correct. This keeps the client supported, protects patient care, and follows the CSR role.

options.text: Suggest using half.
options.feedback: This may seem faster in the moment, but it creates communication, safety, privacy, or trust problems.

blocks.content: Some dog products can be unsafe for cats. The CSR must never guess about using products across species or changing dose.
BLUEEE clinic reality: the goal is not to memorize a script word-for-word. The goal is to understand the decision behind the script so the learner can adapt professionally in real situations.

blocks.question: What is the safest product advice?

options.text: The fastest answer, even if details are missing
options.feedback: Fast is not helpful if it creates errors or unsafe assumptions.

options.text: Only use products as approved for that pet and route safety questions.
options.feedback: Correct.

options.text: Tell the client someone else will deal with it and move on
options.feedback: Passing the problem away without a useful handoff damages trust and workflow.

blocks.back: Never use dog products on cats unless specifically approved.

blocks.content: Future media: use a purposeful photo, diagram, role-play video, or checklist only when it improves learning; avoid decorative filler.
Practice task: learner should explain out loud what they would say, what they would document, and when they would escalate this situation.
